details.text: Derivative: أَنْزَلْنَا “We sent down”. There are two words used for the sending down of the Qur’an from the same root: أَنْزَلَ - إِنْزَال and نَزَّلَ – تَنْزِيل. أَنْزَلَ in the أَفْعَلَ (Af’ala) form pattern implies an act that happens in one go. نَزَّلَ upon the فَعَّلَ (Fa’’ala) form pattern implies something that takes place over time. The use of أَنْزَلْنَا here supports the view that this sending down of the Qur’an refers to its descent in one go from Al-Lawh Al-Mahfoozh (The Preserved Tablet) to the lowest heaven.

إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ

- Concerning the use of the plural form of “We” in إِنَّا (No doubt, We) and أَنْزَلْنَا (We revealed), then Allah is mentioned in the Qur’an with four pronouns, هُوَ (He), أَنْتَ (You), أَنَا (I) and نَحْنُ (We). All are singular apart from “We”. The second and third person forms “He and You” are never used with the plural form and only the first person is “I and We”. In Arabic and other languages, the “We” form is used to portray standing, formality, royalty and power even when it is referring to the singular form. The use of “We” is used often in the Qur’an especially in relation to Allah’s creation and power. It is often used in respect to the sending down of rain and revelation. “We” is used in the language for formal declarations. However, “I” is used in the emotive statements in the Qur’an in relation to extreme punishment or extreme mercy.

- لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ is commonly translated as the “Night of power” which is one of the meanings of قَدْر (Qadr). قَدْر comes from قَدَرَ which also carries the meaning of estimation and determination. As such it has been said that it is called لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (Laylat ul-Qadr) because Allah determines what He wills from His command for the year ahead in it i.e., the Angels are informed of Allah’s decree on that night. It has also been said that it is called لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (Laylat ul-Qadr) due to its great honour and standing, in the case where قَدْر (Qadr) carries the meaning of worth and standing. A third view is that it has been called لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (Laylat ul-Qadr) because it is the night of appreciation in respect to the worship undertaken by Allah’s servants on that night being most appreciated and also that the servants can appreciate their own worth on that night, which is based on قَدْر (Qadr) also carrying the meaning of appreciation and worth. Another meaning of قَدْر (Qadr) is to constrain and tighten and based on this it has been said that لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (Laylat ul-Qadr) refers to the night where space becomes tight and constricted due to so many Angels descending on that night. It can be said that the use of قَدْر allows for all these meanings to be encompassed within a single word without any compromising the meanings of others.

- One of the uses of مَا in the Arabic language is for the purpose of تَعَجُّب (Ta’ajjub – To provide a sense of awe and amazement) and تَفْخِيم (Tafkheem – To give something gravity). وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ could therefore be understood to mean: “What in the world could make you realise, know or possibly give you a clue …”.
- مَا أَدْرَاكَ uses the past tense of this phrase and مَا يُدْريكَ is its present tense form. When مَا يُدْريكَ is used in the Qur’an the answer to the question is not provided and refers to something that only Allah knows. When مَا أَدْرَاكَ is used Allah usually provides the answer and explanation.

لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ

- This is the only place in the Qur’an where the subject of the question form وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ is repeated in the following verse. لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ is the subject of the question form وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ and here the answer begins by repeating لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ. This provides لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ with an emphasis and gravity greater than that of other instances when وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ is used.
- أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ literally means “One thousand months”. It was also used by Arabs as a phrase to indicate to an infinite amount of time i.e., a time without limits.

Derivative: تَنَزَّلُ in origin is تَتَنَزَّلُ but has had one of the Taa’s (تاء) omitted. This shortened form has been argued to imply something that happens less than if the full form is used. Here it relates to an occurrence that happens on one night. In verse 30 of Surah Al-Fussilat the full form of تَتَنَزَّلُ is used and it refers to an occurrence that happens constantly. The shortened form تَنَزَّلُ has also been said to imply that the Angels and Ruh descend very quickly as the word is pronounced quicker.

تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ

- It is observed that when the Angels and Ruh (Jibreel (as)) are mentioned with movement in the Qur’an, the Angels (الْمَلَائِكَةُ) are mentioned first. In Surah An-Naba’ verse 38 the Ruh (الرُّوحُ) is mentioned before the Angels (الْمَلَائِكَةُ) because it is in the context of standing and status.
- مِنْ is commonly translated as “from” however it can have a variety of meanings in classical Arabic usage. One is that it can be حَرْف أَجَل (A preposition implying purpose and reasoning). As such مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ here can imply that the Angels have been given permission to descend “because of every single command” that Allah has issued for them for that coming year. مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ can also linguistically imply “With all sorts of commands”.
